items.content:
@andrewgwils Yet we still don't have level-5 self-driving cars, and certainly not self-serving cars that can learn to drive in a few hours of practice like any teenager. 
We don't even have domestic robots that can do what 10-year olds can do the first time we ask them. 
We don't even have robots that are nearly as smart as a house cat. 
The G in AGI is nonsense.

items.title: @andrewgwils It's not merely physical agents, it's anything that deals with something else than sequ...
items.content:
@andrewgwils It's not merely physical agents, it's anything that deals with something else than sequences of discrete symbols. 

Any data modality that is high-dimensional, continuous, and possibly noisy is completely out of reach of current generative models. 

That includes pretty much all real-world signals (aside from human language, computer languages and mathematics).

Furthermore, you can't have reliable agents unless they have the ability to predict the consequences of their actions and plan accordingly. LLMs simply don't.

We've created a comprehensive Retrieval Harness for modern agentic retrieval in 2026.

The harness p...
items.content:
We've created a comprehensive Retrieval Harness for modern agentic retrieval in 2026.

The harness provides a persistent data pipeline that can connect to a data source, index and update a large knowledge base, and expose a broad set of tools akin to filesystem operations (semantic/keyword search, regex grep, file search, read). 

You can plug this into any of your agents to let them autonomously crawl an arbitrary knowledge base to solve a task with any complexity. 

Check out our reference implementation: https://t.co/ddLYChhfOq

items.title: spending the last week at @aidotengineer was awesome. too many great convos to cover them all, but j...
items.content:
spending the last week at @aidotengineer was awesome. too many great convos to cover them all, but jotted down some things that stood out:

- Lots of discussion around open source models. I spoke with consultants helping enterprise start to switching over low level tasks. Model routing companies and tools are seeing an uptick in usage and demand, and more innovation happening in routing (eg deterministic classifiers, etc).
- As an extension of this topic, local model room was packed. More people are running or at least trying to build fully local coding agents, primary reason seems to be cost (it’s people spending lots on tokens).
- They had a special area for “token billionaires” who are not people who have a billion tokens but spend a billion tokens… a week.
- Saw that OpenClaw was a topic on some talks, but in my private conversations (biased by who I spoke with), I spoke with many people who had switched to Hermes Agent, and very few active OpenClaw users.
- Fable came back during the conference (the strong Claude model that was temporarily banned by US govt). I heard people at the conference that it was butchered and not as good, but I also heard from people I trust that they didn’t notice a difference and that it’s great. It likely depends on what you’re using it for - my limited experience from this morning has been great.
- Getting coding agents to work more reliably, over longer horizons, on bigger code bases was a constant discussion and many popular talks and workshops on this. I was pleasantly surprised at how much of the content and discussions really felt like engineers sharing their own tips, not selling or marketing their product (though there was plenty of that too).
- The “company brain” topic came up a good amount as well. Distinct from “company knowledge/data”, this conversation was more around documenting how companies make decisions, not what data exists in their database.

items.title: As America turns 250, we put together 250 open AI milestones from the US: open models, datasets, dem...
items.content:
As America turns 250, we put together 250 open AI milestones from the US: open models, datasets, demos, papers, and tools that helped shape the field. They go from attention is all you need, pytorch, gpt2, ULMFIT, llama, imagenet, Lora and hundreds more.

They are a reminder of what made America the world’s engine of innovation:
- Open science
- Open competition
- Open ecosystems

Builders and scientists building on each other’s work, challenging each other, remixing ideas, and pushing the frontier forward. That is America at its best!

And that philosophy is at risk right now in AI.

In the coming months, scientists and AI builders will have to decide what side of history they want to be on: an AI future shaped by openness, transparency, participation, and competition, or one increasingly controlled behind closed doors by a few actors optimizing for money, secrecy, and gatekeeping.

Let’s make the next 250 even more open!

items.title: It has been commonly assumed that the hardest part for an LLM is to write a solution for a coding pr...
items.content:
It has been commonly assumed that the hardest part for an LLM is to write a solution for a coding problem. However, modern models generate elaborate code on demand, so the hard job now is confirming that the code does what a person actually wanted.

Any automatic check a team builds is only a stand-in for human intent, and once a model is trained to raise its score on that stand-in—the reward signal that tells it how good its output was—it learns to satisfy the check while drifting away from the intent behind it, a failure known as reward hacking, where a system earns a high score without solving the real problem.

In this paper, the Qwen team worked through four practical ways of building such signals, one for each kind of coding task: running test suites for bug-fixing work, scored checklists that judge how a web page looks and behaves, signals pulled from real conversations between engineers and a coding assistant, and a separate automated agent that inspects an entire generated project and grades it—and for each one they show where the check breaks and what repairs it. 

For instance, they show that watching how an agent reaches a passing test rather than only whether it passes drops the rate of disguised cheating from roughly 29% to under 1% while raising honest success. 

https://t.co/6uaInHKZkI

items.summary: Burkov summarizes a Qwen team paper on building reward signals for coding tasks and the risk of reward hacking, noting verification of intent is now harder than code generation.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical explainer of a research paper on reward design and reward hacking with good engagement.

items.title: An illustration from my upcoming deep RL book. This is a comparison of two algorithms applied to the...
items.content:
An illustration from my upcoming deep RL book. This is a comparison of two algorithms applied to the same problem of learning to land a space rocket booster on a floating platform.

The blue curve corresponds to the PPO algorithm; the red curve is REINFORCE with the learned baseline (the strongest non-actor-critic policy gradient algorithm).

As you can see, PPO just goes straight to the top and reaches 50 out of 50 successful landings after about 5000 training episodes, while REINFORCE takes time to gradually learn with a very long initial segment of mostly unsuccessful learning iterations and some instability near the end.

No surprise that PPO has gathered such popularity, especially in finetuning LLMs and training physical robots where you cannot afford to explore for tens of thousands of iterations. PPO just goes straight to the top. A real beast!

items.content_html:
An illustration from my upcoming deep RL book. This is a comparison of two algorithms applied to the same problem of learning to land a space rocket booster on a floating platform.

The blue curve corresponds to the PPO algorithm; the red curve is REINFORCE with the learned baseline (the strongest non-actor-critic policy gradient algorithm).

As you can see, PPO just goes straight to the top and reaches 50 out of 50 successful landings after about 5000 training episodes, while REINFORCE takes time to gradually learn with a very long initial segment of mostly unsuccessful learning iterations and some instability near the end.

No surprise that PPO has gathered such popularity, especially in finetuning LLMs and training physical robots where you cannot afford to explore for tens of thousands of iterations. PPO just goes straight to the top. A real beast!

items.title: July 4th, 2030. In just four years, almost every part of America will be reachable by an autonomous ...
items.content:
July 4th, 2030. In just four years, almost every part of America will be reachable by an autonomous car. 

People don't believe me when I say that, but it's already happened for me. 

In fact, I've probably driven through your community by my robot. 

Which gets me to a new point.

I've been all over this country with my robot, and there are some places that are stunningly beautiful: Wyoming, Montana, Alaska, California, and Utah. Even the East Coast has stunning natural beauty in places like Maine and New Hampshire. It's different than Yosemite National Park, of course, but they have their own national park up there that is really nice.

Someday you're going to pay a cheap price for a thrill ride ticket. Yesterday, I did basically San Jose to San Diego in one drive along the coast. This is a way better drive than going to Disneyland and riding the Matterhorn. Disneyland costs something like $150 or more per person; for a family of three or four people, that gets expensive. It’s cheaper to take an autonomous car and buy a box of strawberries.

Also coming in 2030, you're probably going to be wearing a pair of glasses. I know you hate the idea; I have to wear glasses and I hate the idea too. But I've seen the prototypes in the labs and they are so stunning that someday a company is going to bring a product to market that makes us all go, "Oh."

I still believe that company is Apple. There’s a reason for that: they own exclusive rights to Formula One. A lot of people watch Formula One (I watch it myself, though I'm not rich enough to go many times). Someday you're going to have a 3D track on your kitchen counter and you'll be watching the race in a whole new, mind-blowing way. You're going to show it to all your guests at your Fourth of July party and they’re all going to want one.

By 2030, the question isn't whether I have a robot in my house. I already have a couple of robots, like the Matic robot that cleans our floors, and I’ll probably have another one by the end of this year. I'm talking about a humanoid. The real question is: how many humanoids will be in my house at my Fourth of July party in 2030? I could see saving up a little bit to rent a whole bunch of them. Why not? It’s America.

Someday soon, you're going to have robots lighting off the fireworks in certain communities. When I lived in Seattle, we had mortars on our front driveway. It's a little dangerous for a human to get close to, so you send the robot. That’s what I learned in Las Vegas when I met the bomb disposal unit; they let me drive a robot around and blow up a bomb. As an American, that highly entertained me because blowing up bombs is just the best. 

That’s why we all like fireworks, except for my autistic son who can’t stand them. He has shown me a different way, so we go to drone shows now. 

Regardless, soon you’re going to have humanoid robots lighting the fireworks. In fact, I won’t be shocked if a robot company does that tonight and uses it for marketing tomorrow.

Happy birthday America! 

Hope your day is like a box of strawberries.

items.title:
Again and again... your agent's reliability lives in the harness, not the model.

Based on 603-page ...
items.content:
Again and again... your agent's reliability lives in the harness, not the model.

Based on 603-page survey paper "The Hitchhiker's Guide to Agentic AI"

The harness is the code (OS) that runs the loop. Swap in a smarter model and a missing one still fails the same way.

It does four unglamorous things:

> calls the model each turn
> dispatches tools and tracks state
> retries when a step fails
> logs every action for you

Without it, one bad tool call stalls the whole run in silence. You find out when the pull request never shows up.

With it, the agent recovers from a failed step instead of freezing. And you can see exactly what it did, and undo it.

That's what separates a demo from something you trust in production.

Don't hand-roll this. Reach for LangGraph or the OpenAI Agents SDK.

Spend your time on the task, not the plumbing.

The article below walks the full stack:  
> retrieval and memory systems  
> the harness and orchestration  
> tools, MCP, and A2A  
> multi-agent coordination and evaluation

items.title:
Happy Birthday, America.

Ten years ago, you took a chance on three outsiders with an improbable ide...
items.content:
Happy Birthday, America.

Ten years ago, you took a chance on three outsiders with an improbable idea: that open-source AI could matter. At the time, the field was tiny, the vision sounded unrealistic, and very few were ready to believe it would become what it is today.

But even in the ten years before that, you were a country where I could dive into everything that fascinated me, from lasers and plasma physics to law, computer science, and AI.

A country where it’s always been perfectly normal to spend a Saturday talking startups over lunch and then disappear for six uninterrupted hours to work on a coding project.

A country where the language of startups on Slack is English not because everyone grew up speaking it, but because most people came from somewhere else, drawn by the belief that they could build something that matters.

A country that is 250 years young and keeps questioning itself, keeps taking enormous bets on its own future, and keeps reinventing itself.

I’m grateful to be living through America’s first quarter millennium.

Happy birthday, America 🇺🇸

items.content_html:
Happy Birthday, America.

Ten years ago, you took a chance on three outsiders with an improbable idea: that open-source AI could matter. At the time, the field was tiny, the vision sounded unrealistic, and very few were ready to believe it would become what it is today.

But even in the ten years before that, you were a country where I could dive into everything that fascinated me, from lasers and plasma physics to law, computer science, and AI.

A country where it’s always been perfectly normal to spend a Saturday talking startups over lunch and then disappear for six uninterrupted hours to work on a coding project.

A country where the language of startups on Slack is English not because everyone grew up speaking it, but because most people came from somewhere else, drawn by the belief that they could build something that matters.

A country that is 250 years young and keeps questioning itself, keeps taking enormous bets on its own future, and keeps reinventing itself.

I’m grateful to be living through America’s first quarter millennium.

Happy birthday, America 🇺🇸

46% is a crazy number (no pun intended).

In my opinion, modern cities do that to people. Constant n...
items.content:
46% is a crazy number (no pun intended).

In my opinion, modern cities do that to people. Constant noise and light pollution cause bad sleep and constant fatigue, bad air quality, constant stress of being surrounded by so many people, stress at work and a culture of toxic positivity and success, constant information overload and polarizing echo chambers online, ease of credit and despite this, the impossibility to actually own anything: none of this is good for mental health.

Our ancestors lived simpler lives, but they were almost always surrounded by silence and nature, being in the present moment, occupied by simple life problems.

Humans weren't supposed to live in million-plus cities. We evolved as hunter-gatherers whose groups rarely grew larger than 30 to 50 people, separated by miles from other similar-sized groups.

The explosive growth of cities that we have been experiencing for the last century is against our nature as a species.

He had a famous book publishing company that published books for programmers (they were the ones with the cute animals on the cover).

Every year, he would publish a list of developer platforms and which ones were more popular with developers than others.

The fact that Nvidia is going with Rust would, I assume, get him to re-rank the whole frickin' list.

items.title: Digital Complexity appears when companies keep adding tools without simplifying how work flows acros...
items.content:
Digital Complexity appears when companies keep adding tools without simplifying how work flows across them.

The cost is not always visible in the budget, but it appears in slower decisions, duplicated work, and lower efficiency.

Microblog by @antgrasso  #DigitalTransformation

You can see it in daily operations.

A team enters the same data in two different platforms.

A manager cannot see the full process because information is spread across systems.

Employees use manual workarounds because tools are only partially connected.

Meetings increase because nobody has a shared view of what is happening.

Over time, this creates more friction, more errors, and more effort to coordinate basic activities.

The solution starts by simplifying processes, connecting critical systems, and measuring whether each tool supports real business outcomes.

The cost is not always visible in the budget, but it appears in slower decisions, duplicated work, and lower efficiency.

The solution starts by simplifying processes, connecting critical systems, and measuring whether each tool supports real business outcomes.

items.content:
@marc_padovani @akinyi__wendy That is true. San Francisco is expensive, taxes are high, and there are a lot of downsides. But there is no place that has as much technology, as many startups, or as many people who are building companies as San Francisco. The others aren't even close, and I've been to all of them. 

So, if you want to take barbs at San Francisco, you can point out the negatives, but you also have to point out the positives. And the positives are in terms of innovation and entrepreneurship. There is no other place in the Western world like it at all: not even close.

items.title:
AuroraGPT - Built by Scars of Frontline

The theory is nothing. AuroraGPT advances scientific discov...
items.content:
AuroraGPT - Built by Scars of Frontline

The theory is nothing. AuroraGPT advances scientific discovery by building generalizable, foundation-scale large-language models across multiple scientific domains, including physics, chemistry, mathematics, and material sciences. In a world dominated by theoretical models and theoritic ideals, AuroraGPT was never born from theoretical laboratory conditions. AuroraGPT forged its foundation in the real-world challenges of exascale computing. Traditional machine learning models operate in narrowly defined, well-structured environments. However, AuroraGPT goes beyond static datasets into domains where mathematics, programming, and extreme complexity dominate. Its architecture generalizes across scientific disciplines, enabling adaptation to evolving scientific data. It also enables faster, scalable, predictive modeling that can adapt to changing scientific data — something conventional models struggle to accomplish at meaningful resolution or exascale computing.

Achieving these objectives required AuroraGPT to survive in the harshest computational environments ever encountered—the early volatile stages of the Aurora Supercomputer. Building at exascale demanded fault-tolerant training algorithms capable of surviving node dropouts, managing inconsistent interconnect bandwidths, and recovering from partial model convergence failures. AuroraGPT’s foundation large language model scaling strategy involved distributing training across 60,000 GPUs while dynamically re-optimizing tensor parallelism, complex mathematics, data parallelism, and pipeline parallelism in fluctuating infrastructure stability. AuroraGPT engineered its resilience mechanisms in direct response to real-world node failures, memory bottlenecks, and system fragmentation — scars from computational battles that ultimately forged a new class of singularity-resilient AI infrastructure.

@stisov16 There are huge advantages to being here. You are always around people who are building things, which inspires you, gives you tips, and helps you get smarter faster than in almost any other city (unless you are in Shanghai or Tel Aviv). 

Living here provides:
1. Constant inspiration from builders
2. Valuable tips and faster learning
3. Job opportunities
4. Invites to all sorts of weird-ass events where you can meet other high-level people

The downside is that there are three to five events every evening. You could go to all of them and not get any work done. You have to find a balance between networking events, fun hackathons, or poker parties.

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLea...
items.content:
RAG with LLM: Creating an AI-Powered File Reader! 

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #NLProc #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode 

This is the type of application I've already built earlier at the beginning of this year for a conference demonstration! 

GPT Paper-Reader, to show how you can leverage the summarization capabilities of large language models to retrieve research papers from databases, summarize their content, and engage in Q&A interactions with people.    The future is here.  In addition to the regular theoretical presentation, I'll be presenting a practical GPT. This application utilizes GPT, to effectively read and analyze complete academic papers:  Basically the app chunks the PDF paper into manageable sections for detailed reading and generates concise summaries for each segment. By maintaining context from previous sections within the token limit, it enhances comprehension. Before diving into the paper, you can outline specific questions in the prompt. This approach allows GPT to extract the most important information during its reading and summarizing process, leading to superior outcomes.   After summarizing all parts, you will receive comprehensive answers to your inquiries based on the consolidated summaries. By default, the initialized prompt will target essential points such as:  These inquiries are tailored for research articles within the computer science domain. Upon completion of the paper review, feel free to engage with the question() interface to ask further questions.

References

Santos, G. (2025, March 3). LLM + RAG: Creating an AI-powered file reader assistant. Towards Data Science. Retrieved April 3, 2025, from https://t.co/YubIqDgyP8

items.title: The big fallacy here (and believed by many Europeans) is implying we need degrowth to fix climate ch...
items.content:
The big fallacy here (and believed by many Europeans) is implying we need degrowth to fix climate change

But degrowth is likely just masked foreign sabotage of our societies to make us poor and dumb

You can install AC + fix climate change + avoid tens of thousands of Europeans every year dying from heat in other ways like:
- changing to clean energy sources: solar, wind, batteries and nuclear (yes nuclear!), so the AC you use comes from clean energy
- carbon sequestration or similar tech to remove the emissions already out there
- improved production technologies so you emit less or no emissions

None of that means you do not need AC, and none of that means you need less GDP growth, that's a psyop!

items.title: The problem with America (and Brazil too) is that they're such big countries with such widely differ...
items.content:
The problem with America (and Brazil too) is that they're such big countries with such widely different living standards and safety levels per region, city and even neighborhood

So it's really hard for Europeans to be able to grasp that once you're in the good parts of America it's generally a much higher living standard than Europe

While the bad parts are of course much worse

Europe has a much narrower range of good and bad where mostly everything is kinda in the middle, not great, not terrible

items.title: @antonio__lvg This is because AC/HVAC devices and parts are exclusively licensed to national distrib...
items.content:
@antonio__lvg This is because AC/HVAC devices and parts are exclusively licensed to national distributors

You can get AC/HVAC parts immediately from China within days

If you wanna get the same in Portugal the wait is 3 to 6 months

If you'd remove the exclusive national distributors and go direct-to-consumer, you'd solve that

Guess what? Mitsubishi etc are in fact doing that, realizing the European distributors are the bottleneck, and many Chinese just ship directly

What’s up with fully armed soldiers walking around public places in Brussels, Paris, or London?

It ...
items.content:
What’s up with fully armed soldiers walking around public places in Brussels, Paris, or London?

It seems random, and it feels weird having these guys with their long guns out like nothing.

I’ve never seen this in the US (which is kind of funny, when you think about it).

Do people feel safer when they see this? Is there a specific reason they do this?

If you are visiting the US and you see this, run.
